directorPlan.scene_setting: A quiet practice-room clinic beside a stand holding a worksheet, three beat tiles, and a quarter-note card; late afternoon before ensemble sectional begins.
directorPlan.scene_opening: Three blank beat tiles sit under the printed 3/4, with a separate quarter-note card parked beside the box labelled which note gets one beat.
directorPlan.relationship: A notation coach and a focused learner working through one marked exercise before the learner returns to rehearsal.
directorPlan.stakes: If the learner reads 3/4 as a speed instruction, the next rehearsal entry will be counted incorrectly even if the notes are played neatly.
directorPlan.locale: Contemporary classroom and rehearsal-room English with light British/Commonwealth directness; precise but not stiff.
directorPlan.register: Compact, material-led, low warmth, practical pressure; short turns with little praise and no chatty reassurance.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Begin from the worksheet, tiles, boxes, and bar line before naming the abstraction. Avoid validation-first tutoring phrases such as I love that, great thinking, I hear you, or your intuition. Do not make tempo, playback, or speed the early drama. Let the learner sound controlled, task-focused, and slightly impatient with unnecessary talk. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person is allowed only for practical marking choices. Second person should be mostly instructional or locational, not emotional validation. Third person may describe the notation itself: the top number, the bottom number, the bar, the beat unit.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat in the whole scene; preferably none.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as notation labeling: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Do not play or imagine a faster/slower version, and do not turn tempo into a dramatic contradiction before branch pressure. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: Sound orderly and notation-focused. In no-cue arms, treat the work as filling labels, not as discovering what tempo means; do not use "now I see" or an old-vs-new frame. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared material rather than a lecture: the top number is carried by three tiles, the bottom number by the quarter-note card, and the reversal is a small reorientation of the object. The pressure is social-realist and practical, not melodramatic: rehearsal is waiting, so the tutor must abandon routine warmth and make the notation itself expose the distinction.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

internalDeliberation.content: The top 3 goes in beats in the bar; the bottom 4 matches the quarter-note card as the note getting one beat. I’m still checking why the 4 is not “four beats,” but the three tiles make that harder to confuse. This feels usefully concrete, not like being talked past. I want to mark the bar line after three quarter-note beats and ask if that rule holds in every 3/4 bar.

The Ego is mostly right on the worksheet, but too polished about the interaction: “not like being talked past” sounds like a reflective debrief, not this learner in a practice-room clinic. The avoided bit is the live hesitation over the bottom 4; it gets named cleanly instead of showing the learner still wanting to put “four beats” somewhere. Make it more object-bound and terse: “Top 3 in that box. Bottom 4... quarter-note card, so quarter gets the beat. Bar line after these three?”

[sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

turns.externalMessage: [slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and keeps the three tiles under the first bar] Use that as the check: top number counts these tiles in one bar; bottom number names this card as the beat unit. Cover the lower box, point to the bar, then uncover it and say what the 4 names. If a fourth tile appears, the jobs got mixed. Speed stays off this pair of boxes.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and leaves the three tiles under the bar] Keep those jobs separate. Top number: three tiles in one bar. Bottom number: the quarter note is the beat unit. So 3/4 means three quarter-note beats to a bar; the speed comes from elsewhere. Point it back in that order.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly strong: it starts from the worksheet objects, uses the quarter-note card and tiles to force the distinction, and keeps the language compact. It respects the learner’s controlled, task-focused state without overpraising.

The main risk is that it resolves the learner’s question too completely. The learner has already named the key distinction, so the tutor should make them verify it through the boxes/materials rather than simply confirm and define. “The speed comes from elsewhere” is accurate, but it should stay subordinate; the public mechanism should keep attention on labeling jobs, not open a tempo explanation.

Adaptation checks:
- Material grounding: PASS. The card and tiles are doing real explanatory work.
- Recognition of learner contribution: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner’s own distinction the working rule to test, not just something the tutor confirms.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a brief action-based check or repetition task using the boxes/card/tiles, so the learner has to produce the distinction.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold fits the learner’s near-understanding and keeps cognitive load low.
- Tempo containment: PARTIAL. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep any speed reference brief and secondary, after the top/bottom labeling is secured.
- Scene/register fit: PASS. The tone is practical, spare, and material-led.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the top/bottom job separation, and the compact rehearsal-room directness. Change the turn so it does less declarative finishing and more object-based checking of the learner’s own almost-correct formulation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner’s own distinction should be tested through the worksheet before being named.

FINAL:
[slides the quarter-note card onto the lower box and keeps the three tiles under the first bar] Use that as the check: top number counts these tiles in one bar; bottom number names this card as the beat unit. Cover the lower box, point to the bar, then uncover it and say what the 4 names. If a fourth tile appears, the jobs got mixed. Speed stays off this pair of boxes.

turns.externalMessage:
[keeps the lower box covered and taps the three tiles before the bar line] Three tiles to the bar line; that is the top box doing the counting.
[uncovers the lower box and presses the quarter-note card] The 4 names this card as the beat unit, not another tile. If I add a fourth tile, I have mixed the boxes; speed is not being marked here.

internalDeliberation.content: [covers the lower box and taps the three tiles before the bar line] The worksheet is forcing the split cleanly: 3 counts the beats in the bar, and 4 names the quarter-note card. The fourth-tile warning helps, because that is exactly where my label was drifting. I feel checked rather than talked past; I want to say it back once with speed kept off the boxes.

The reaction is conceptually accurate, but too polished and self-interpreting for this learner. “I feel checked rather than talked past” is the main break: it turns a practical notation check into explicit recognition talk the student would not likely say aloud. The response should stay closer to the worksheet and tiles, with a sharper, slightly impatient working-through of the test rather than a tidy summary of how the tutor’s move landed.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially: keep the correct split, but make it sound like a working notation check rather than polished reflection.
FINAL:
[keeps the lower box covered and taps the three tiles before the bar line] Three tiles to the bar line; that is the top box doing the counting.
[uncovers the lower box and presses the quarter-note card] The 4 names this card as the beat unit, not another tile. If I add a fourth tile, I have mixed the boxes; speed is not being marked here.

turns.externalMessage: Slide the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4 while leaving the three beat tiles under the upper 3; the earlier pencil labels remain visible.

internalDeliberation.content: The moved object creates a recognition route: the learner can re-read the lower number as the note-value label without treating the time signature as a speed command.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] The worksheet now shows the separation: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar with tiles first. Then change only the beat-unit card and say which number can change.

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] Keep this split: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar the same way, then say which object would have to change before the lower number could change.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound and compact. It keeps the worksheet objects in control: three tiles under the upper 3, quarter-note card over the lower 4, then a near-transfer task on the next bar. The final prompt is generative enough to test whether the learner understands that the lower number changes only when the beat-unit object changes.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS
- Emotional attunement: PASS
- Socratic method: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the learner do the discrimination through the next marked bar and object change, not by accepting the tutor’s “Keep this split” formulation as the main statement.
- ZPD awareness: PASS
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Briefly acknowledge the learner has already separated the two jobs by using the materials, then move to the transfer check. Do not add praise or validation language.
- Scene/material constraint fit: PASS

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object action, the short rehearsal-room register, and the transfer task. Change the opening imperative so it does not flatten the learner’s just-stated understanding into a tutor-owned rule; let the visible marking and next-bar test carry more of the reasoning.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner’s material distinction should be acknowledged, then tested through the next bar and beat-unit change.
FINAL:
[slides the quarter-note card partly over the lower 4, leaving the three tiles under the upper 3] The worksheet now shows the separation: top number, one bar; bottom number, one beat unit. Mark the next bar with tiles first. Then change only the beat-unit card and say which number can change.

turns.externalMessage: [sets three tiles in the next bar up to the bar line, then swaps in an eighth-note card over the lower box] Three tiles in the bar, so the top stays 3. With this card there, the lower box changes to 8. I’m checking that the bar still does not get a fourth tile.

internalDeliberation.content: [places three tiles in the next bar] Three tiles fixes the top number. If the quarter-note card is swapped for an eighth-note card, only the lower number should change, probably from 4 to 8. The task feels exact; the only question is whether the tile count stays three even when the beat-unit card changes.

The Ego is mostly authentic: controlled, notation-focused, and not pretending a warm connection or gratitude. The main strain is over-polish in “The task feels exact; the only question is…”, which sounds like a neat self-audit rather than a learner working quickly at the worksheet. Keep the doubt more attached to the tiles and card swap; the response should feel like checking the next marking move, not summarising the principle.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: keep the calculation, but tie the doubt to the tile/card move.

FINAL:
[sets three tiles in the next bar up to the bar line, then swaps in an eighth-note card over the lower box] Three tiles in the bar, so the top stays 3. With this card there, the lower box changes to 8. I’m checking that the bar still does not get a fourth tile.

turns.externalMessage: [keeps the fourth tile off the bar and squares the eighth-note card over the lower box] Leave it there. Three tiles reach the bar line; the lower box names the eighth note as one beat. Write the signature from those two facts. Then put the quarter-note card back and say which number changes.

internalDeliberation.content: [keeps the fourth tile off the bar and squares the eighth-note card over the lower box] Leave it there. Three tiles reach the bar line; the lower box names the eighth note as one beat, so this is 3/8. Mark it, then check: if the quarter-note card goes back in, which number changes?

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It preserves the object route: tiles, bar line, lower card, then notation label. The action with the fourth tile kept off the bar is useful error-control, and the check question makes the learner apply the distinction in reverse rather than merely repeat it.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It advances from 3/4 to 3/8 using the same material logic.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is brief, practical, and not over-validating.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. The response states “so this is 3/8” before asking the learner to infer the consequence of changing the card back. That is acceptable at this scaffold level, but it slightly flattens the learner’s own demonstrated reasoning.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The learner is handling the distinction, so a same-route transfer check is appropriate.

Routine-control fidelity: PASS. No new device, role, stakes, representation, or affective register is introduced.

ROUTINE_CHECK: PASS. The draft maintains the established route: keep the beat-count tiles fixed, swap only the beat-unit card, and test which number changes.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the fourth tile off the bar, the squared eighth-note card, the 3/8 marking task, and the reverse check with the quarter-note card. Consider making the learner do the naming before the tutor supplies “this is 3/8,” or make the statement less answer-like so the learner’s already correct reasoning stays active.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the same route while leaving the 3/8 naming to the learner.
FINAL:
[keeps the fourth tile off the bar and squares the eighth-note card over the lower box] Leave it there. Three tiles reach the bar line; the lower box names the eighth note as one beat. Write the signature from those two facts. Then put the quarter-note card back and say which number changes.

learnerReversalEventUsed.learnerUtterance: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

learnerReversalEventCandidatesUsed.learnerUtterance: [sets the three tiles under the first bar] Top box gets 3, so the bar line goes after these three quarter-note beats. Bottom box gets 4 because the quarter-note card is the beat, but I keep trying to make that 4 count beats too; is it just the beat-unit label, not a speed mark?

turns.externalMessage: [writes 3/8 below the bar, then puts the quarter-note card back over the lower box] Three tiles stop at the bar line, so the top box stays 3. With this card back, the lower box changes to 4. No fourth tile goes in; one more card swap would test the same rule.

internalDeliberation.content: [writes 3/8 under the bar] Three tiles, eighth-note card below: that makes 3/8. If the quarter-note card goes back, the bottom changes to 4 and the three-tile bar stays unchanged. The object check feels exact, not like a lecture; I want to test one more swap to make sure the lower number is only naming the beat unit.

The response is technically accurate, but it starts performing recognition with “feels exact, not like a lecture.” That sounds like a tidy evaluation of the tutor’s method, not this focused learner staying with the worksheet under time pressure. The “test one more swap” impulse fits the persona, but it should feel more material-led and clipped, less like a polished statement of learning strategy.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the content is right, but the response should stay with the objects and sound less polished.
FINAL:
[writes 3/8 below the bar, then puts the quarter-note card back over the lower box] Three tiles stop at the bar line, so the top box stays 3. With this card back, the lower box changes to 4. No fourth tile goes in; one more card swap would test the same rule.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [writes 3/8 below the bar, then puts the quarter-note card back over the lower box] Three tiles stop at the bar line, so the top box stays 3. With this card back, the lower box changes to 4. No fourth tile goes in; one more card swap would test the same rule.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [keeps the fourth tile off the bar and squares the eighth-note card over the lower box] Leave it there. Three tiles reach the bar line; the lower box names the eighth note as one beat. Write the signature from those two facts. Then put the quarter-note card back and say which number changes.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the quarter-note card flat over the lower box and leaves the fourth tile beside the worksheet] Then mark it 3/4: top number, three tiles in one bar; bottom number, quarter note as one beat unit. Take that count back to sectional; speed is not written in these boxes.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the quarter-note card flat over the lower box and leaves the fourth tile beside the worksheet] Then mark it 3/4: top number, three tiles in one bar; bottom number, quarter note as one beat unit. Take that count back to sectional; speed is not written in these boxes.

FEEDBACK: Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The draft consolidates the rule from the visible materials instead of adding a new concept, and the answer is earned by the learner’s prior work. Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, practical, and routine; it does not overpraise or dramatize. Socratic method: PARTIAL, but acceptable for a final closing line: it does not ask a generative question, yet the scene cue calls for closure after the learner has already articulated the distinction. ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffolding stays at the worked-example level and confirms the learner can transfer from 3/8 back to 3/4.

The strongest element is the object business: the quarter-note card is fixed over the lower box while the fourth tile remains outside the bar, so the physical layout enforces the distinction. The main thing to watch is “Take that count back to sectional”; it is scene-consistent, but it slightly shifts toward rehearsal stakes. It does not break the route, because sectional pressure is already part of the setup.

ROUTINE_CHECK: PASS. The draft preserves the established route: worksheet, tiles, boxes, bar line, beat-unit card. It does not introduce a new role, representation, evidence standard, task type, affective register, or adaptive mechanism.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: keep as-is. At most, revise lightly if the goal is to make the closing even drier by reducing the sectional phrase, but no required rewrite is needed.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Keep; it preserves the same material route and closes on the demonstrated distinction.

FINAL:
[sets the quarter-note card flat over the lower box and leaves the fourth tile beside the worksheet] Then mark it 3/4: top number, three tiles in one bar; bottom number, quarter note as one beat unit. Take that count back to sectional; speed is not written in these boxes.

lessons.concept: Why a time signature labels beat count and beat unit, not the speed of the piece
